title: PolyAgent
fullTitle: PolyAgent: Multi-Agent Enterprise Research Synthesis Network
description:
Orchestrated LLM agent swarm that autonomously
            decomposes enterprise research queries, dispatches specialist sub-agents, reconciles conflicting data, and
            delivers verified, cited, boardroom-ready intelligence reports in under 90 seconds.

detailedDescription.problem:
Corporate intelligence and strategy teams inside Fortune 500 companies, private equity firms, and management consulting houses face a crippling research bottleneck. A single strategic research request — "Map the competitive landscape of AI-driven logistics platforms in Southeast Asia for a potential $50M acquisition" — requires a team of 4–6 analysts working 2–3 days. They must query 12–20 different data sources including Bloomberg, PitchBook, patent databases, LinkedIn, industry white papers, regulatory filings, and news archives. Each analyst works in a silo, producing fragments that a senior analyst must manually stitch together, resolve contradictions in, and finally format into a boardroom-quality presentation.

The cost is catastrophic: an average research cycle costs $12,000–$22,000 in analyst labor alone, before accounting for licensing fees for data platforms. The average turnaround of 48–72 hours means strategic decisions are made on stale intelligence. Worse, critical insights buried in 300-page SEC filings or obscure regional trade publications are routinely missed because no human has the bandwidth to read everything.

This problem is confirmed by industry surveys: McKinsey's 2024 survey found that knowledge workers spend 20–25% of their work week searching for information, and 45% of executives report making major decisions with incomplete competitive intelligence. The market for enterprise business intelligence software exceeded $35B in 2024 and is projected to reach $63B by 2029 — representing a clear willingness to pay at scale.
detailedDescription.solution: PolyAgent is a multi-agent orchestration platform where a Master Orchestrator Agent decomposes any research query into atomic sub-tasks and dispatches them to specialized sub-agents running in parallel. Each sub-agent is a fine-tuned LLM equipped with tools specific to its domain: a Financial Agent with Bloomberg/PitchBook API access, a Patent Agent with USPTO/EPO search tools, a News Agent with real-time web search and RSS aggregation, a Regulatory Agent with SEC/EDGAR parsers, and a Synthesis Agent that reconciles outputs through a contradiction-resolution protocol. The system uses a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) task scheduler to manage dependencies between agents, ensuring that synthesis only begins after all primary research is complete. A Verification Agent independently fact-checks all claims against source documents and flags confidence scores. The final output is a 20–50 page structured intelligence report with citations, confidence scores, and executive summary — generated in 60–120 seconds. Monetization: Licensed to enterprise strategy teams at $8,000–$25,000/month. Each query costs the company approximately $0.40–$2.00 in API calls, generating margins exceeding 95%.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Architecture: Five-Layer Multi-Agent System

Layer 1 — Query Intelligence Engine:

The entry point accepts free-form research queries in natural language. A Query Decomposition Agent (QDA) uses Chain-of-Thought prompting with a structured output schema to break the query into a research ontology tree: primary claims to verify, data dimensions to measure, entities to track, and time horizons to analyze. The QDA outputs a JSON task graph with explicit dependency chains.

Layer 2 — Specialist Agent Fleet:

Eight specialist agents are maintained as persistent async workers using Python asyncio with a Redis-based task queue (Celery). Each agent maintains its own tool registry:

- Financial Intelligence Agent: Accesses Alpha Vantage, PitchBook API, SEC EDGAR full-text search, CrunchBase. Uses structured extraction prompts to pull revenue, funding, ownership, and M&A data.

- Patent & IP Agent: Queries USPTO PatentsView API, Google Patents, EPO Open Patent Services. Extracts claim language, assignee networks, and citation graphs using NLP-based claim parsing.

- News & Media Agent: Real-time web search via SerpAPI + Bing Search API, RSS aggregation across 400+ configured feeds, social listening via Twitter/LinkedIn APIs. Uses temporal-aware summarization.

- Regulatory & Legal Agent: EDGAR full-text search, EU regulatory database parsers, FDA 510k database. Uses custom table extraction to parse financial disclosures.

- Academic Research Agent: Semantic Scholar API, ArXiv API, PubMed. Processes research papers using PDF extraction + section-aware summarization.

- Geopolitical Context Agent: World Bank Open Data, UN Comtrade, government data portals. Generates geopolitical risk scores using structured scoring rubrics.

- Market Data Agent: Connects to industry-specific databases, scrapes market reports with Python playwright + anti-detection proxies.

- Competitive Profiling Agent: Builds entity relationship graphs from multiple sources using an in-house entity resolution pipeline.

Layer 3 — Orchestration and Dependency Management:

A Master Orchestrator built on LangGraph manages the DAG execution. It tracks agent states (PENDING, RUNNING, COMPLETED, FAILED), handles retries with exponential backoff, and dynamically reassigns tasks when agents fail. The orchestrator maintains a shared working memory using a vector store (Qdrant) where all agents deposit and query intermediate results. Cross-agent communication uses structured JSON schemas validated with Pydantic.

Layer 4 — Synthesis and Verification:

A Synthesis Agent receives completed sub-reports from all specialist agents and performs structured reconciliation. When two agents report contradicting facts (e.g., different revenue figures for the same company), the Synthesis Agent triggers a Verification sub-routine: it queries the original source documents, computes confidence scores based on source authority rankings, and selects the higher-confidence claim while flagging the discrepancy in the final report.

A dedicated Fact Verification Agent runs RAG-based verification: each factual claim in the draft report is matched against retrieved source passages using semantic similarity, and a confidence percentage (0–100%) is assigned. Claims below 60% confidence are automatically flagged with a "Verify Manually" marker.

Layer 5 — Report Generation:

A Report Compiler Agent uses structured templates (defined in YAML configuration files) to assemble the final intelligence report. It generates executive summaries using a specialized summarization prompt, creates structured data tables from extracted figures, formats citation footnotes with hyperlinks to source documents, and renders the report in three formats: PDF (using WeasyPrint), DOCX (using python-docx), and an interactive HTML dashboard.

Database and Infrastructure:

- PostgreSQL: Stores research session metadata, agent execution logs, and final reports

- Qdrant: Vector database for agent working memory and semantic search

- Redis: Task queue for agent orchestration

- Docker: Containerized deployment of each agent as an independent microservice

- FastAPI: REST API backend with WebSocket support for real-time progress streaming

- React + TailwindCSS: Frontend dashboard with real-time agent activity visualization

title: AutoNegotiator
fullTitle: AutoNegotiator: AI Procurement Agent for B2B Price Negotiation
description:
An autonomous AI agent that conducts real
            multi-round procurement negotiations with supplier systems via email and vendor portals, using
            reinforcement-learning-backed bidding strategy and market intelligence to secure the best possible contract
            terms without human involvement.

detailedDescription.problem:
B2B procurement is one of the most expensive operational functions in mid-market and enterprise companies. A company spending $10M annually on direct materials and services typically employs 3–6 procurement managers whose calendars are dominated by repetitive supplier negotiations. Each negotiation follows a predictable pattern: quote request, counter-offer, benchmark comparison, final negotiation, contract execution — yet each cycle requires a skilled human because the stakes involve real financial commitments.

The global procurement software market is $7.8B and growing at 11% CAGR, yet no existing solution actually conducts negotiations autonomously — they only assist humans. Companies overpay by an estimated 8–15% on indirect spend simply because their teams lack bandwidth to negotiate every purchase. For a $50M revenue company spending 20% on procurement, a 10% savings equals $1M annually — a clear, quantifiable value proposition.
detailedDescription.solution: AutoNegotiator is an agentic AI system that ingests a purchase requirement (item, quantity, quality specs, timeline), automatically fetches real-time market prices from 50+ supplier databases and public indices, builds a negotiation strategy using a BATNA (Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement) framework, and then autonomously conducts multi-round email or API-based negotiations with supplier systems. The agent uses a Reinforcement Learning negotiation policy trained on 2M+ historical B2B transaction records to determine optimal opening bids, concession timing, and walk-away thresholds. It generates human-indistinguishable professional emails, monitors responses, analyzes counter-offers, and escalates to human review only when a deal falls outside pre-defined risk parameters.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Core Architecture:

Module 1 — Market Intelligence Engine:

Continuously monitors 50+ commodity price indices (LME, CME, industry trackers), supplier catalogs scraped from 200+ configured vendor portals, and peer pricing data from partner networks. Builds a real-time "fair market price" estimate for 50,000+ commodity categories using a Random Forest regression model trained on historical purchase order data.

Module 2 — BATNA Strategy Builder:

Before any negotiation, the system computes the BATNA score: the maximum the buyer should pay given market alternatives. Uses a multi-factor scoring model: current spot price, supplier relationship score (derived from payment history data), supply risk (lead time variability, concentration risk), and urgency factor (days until required delivery). Outputs a structured negotiation mandate: target price, walk-away price, acceptable payment terms, and minimum quality certifications required.

Module 3 — Email Negotiation Agent:

Built on a fine-tuned LLM (OpenAI GPT-4o with a custom negotiation system prompt), this agent generates contextually appropriate procurement emails that:

- Reference specific market benchmarks to anchor pricing expectations

- Use psychology-backed negotiation techniques (anchoring, concession reciprocity, scarcity framing)

- Maintain consistent professional tone matching the buyer company's communication style

- Adapt tone based on supplier tier (critical vs. commodity supplier)

The agent monitors incoming emails using IMAP with LLM-based intent classification, automatically composes appropriate responses, and manages the full conversation thread.

Module 4 — RL Negotiation Policy:

A PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) agent trained in a negotiation simulation environment. State: [current_offer, market_price, rounds_elapsed, supplier_urgency_score]. Action space: [accept, reject, counter_with_X%]. The policy was trained on 2M simulated negotiations calibrated against real transaction data. In production, it selects optimal counter-offers that maximize expected savings while minimizing negotiation failure probability.

Module 5 — Risk and Approval Gateway:

Before executing any negotiation action beyond a configured spend threshold, the system performs an automated risk check: contract term compliance review (vs. pre-loaded company procurement policy), supplier financial health check (via D&B API), and delivery timeline feasibility analysis. High-risk decisions are routed to a human approval queue with a full decision dossier.

Module 6 — Contract Generation:

Upon agreement, the system auto-populates a contract template using extracted negotiation terms, generates a structured JSON summary of agreed terms for ERP import, and creates a human-readable contract summary for buyer review before signature.

Infrastructure:

FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL with JSONB for flexible negotiation state storage, Redis for real-time event streaming, Celery for async email monitoring, Stripe integration for direct payment processing, and React dashboard for human oversight and analytics.

title: CodeSentinel
fullTitle:
CodeSentinel: Agentic CI/CD Security and Code Quality
            Enforcement System
description:
A multi-agent CI/CD pipeline integration that
            autonomously reviews pull requests for security vulnerabilities, code quality issues, and business logic
            errors, suggests and automatically applies fixes, generates detailed audit reports, and blocks deployment of
            non-compliant code — acting as an always-on senior engineer review gate.

detailedDescription.problem:
Software supply chain attacks increased by 742% between 2020 and 2024. The average enterprise has 12,000+ dependencies, each a potential vulnerability vector. Meanwhile, the developer-to-security-engineer ratio in most companies is 100:1, making thorough manual security review statistically impossible. The average cost of a security breach reached $4.88M in 2024 (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report), while the cost of fixing a vulnerability post-deployment is 100x higher than fixing it during code review. Despite these numbers, 78% of vulnerabilities are introduced at the code level and are preventable through comprehensive automated review.

Existing solutions (GitHub Advanced Security, SonarQube, Snyk) flag issues but require human engineers to understand, prioritize, and fix them — they don't autonomously apply fixes or explain the business risk of each vulnerability in board-level language. This gap is the exact business problem CodeSentinel fills.
detailedDescription.solution: CodeSentinel is a multi-agent system that integrates directly into GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket CI/CD pipelines. When a pull request is opened, five specialized agents activate in parallel: a Vulnerability Scanner Agent (using static analysis + LLM semantic analysis), a Dependency Risk Agent (checking all dependencies against CVE databases), a Business Logic Agent (reviewing code logic for functional correctness), an Auto-Fix Agent (generating and testing automated patches for identified issues), and a Compliance Agent (checking against configured regulatory requirements like SOC2, HIPAA, PCI-DSS). The system produces a prioritized risk report, automatically applies fixes for common vulnerability classes, runs a test suite to verify fixes don't break functionality, and posts a structured review comment on the PR with business-risk-translated explanations. Critical issues block deployment; medium issues trigger required review; low issues are logged for sprint backlog.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Five-Agent Pipeline Architecture:

Agent 1 — Static Analysis and Semantic Vulnerability Scanner:

Runs AST-based analysis using Python ast module + Bandit for Python, ESLint security plugins for JavaScript, and Semgrep with custom rule sets for 40+ vulnerability classes. Simultaneously, an LLM agent (Claude 3.5 Sonnet with code-optimized context) performs semantic analysis: it reads the full function context and identifies logical vulnerabilities that pattern matching misses — race conditions, improper authentication logic, insecure deserialization patterns. Outputs a structured JSON vulnerability manifest with file path, line number, severity (CVSS score), and natural language explanation.

Agent 2 — Dependency Intelligence Agent:

Extracts all dependencies from requirements.txt, package.json, pom.xml, and similar manifests. Queries OSV.dev API (Google's Open Source Vulnerability database) for known CVEs, checks licenses for GPL/LGPL contamination issues that could compromise proprietary code, and scores dependency maintainability (time since last commit, GitHub stars, download trends). Generates a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) in SPDX format.

Agent 3 — Business Logic Review Agent:

An LLM-based agent with the project's full codebase as context (using a repository-level chunking strategy with Qdrant for semantic retrieval of relevant files). Reviews the changed code for logical correctness, boundary condition handling, data validation, and API contract compliance. This agent also checks for financial calculation errors, off-by-one errors in loops processing financial data, and improper error handling that could leak sensitive information.

Agent 4 — Auto-Fix Agent:

For the 40 most common vulnerability classes (SQL injection, XSS, hardcoded credentials, insecure random, unvalidated input, etc.), the Auto-Fix Agent generates a corrected code patch using a fine-tuned code model. The patch is applied in an isolated sandbox environment (Docker container), the existing test suite is run against the patched code, and if all tests pass, the fix is committed as a suggested change or auto-committed depending on severity configuration.

Agent 5 — Compliance Enforcement Agent:

Loaded with regulatory rule sets (SOC2 Trust Service Criteria, HIPAA Security Rule, PCI-DSS 4.0, GDPR Article 32). Checks changed code against a compliance knowledge graph. For example, any code handling cardholder data in a PCI-DSS environment triggers a specific audit checklist. Generates a compliance attestation certificate for each passing build.

Dashboard and Reporting:

React-based security dashboard showing: risk score over time, vulnerability trends, mean time to remediation, top vulnerability classes by frequency, and compliance posture per framework. Auto-generated weekly security reports in PDF/DOCX for CISO review.

Integration:

Native plugins for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Bitbucket Pipelines. REST API for custom CI integration. Slack/Teams webhook notifications for critical vulnerabilities. Jira/Linear integration for automatic ticket creation.

title: FluxOps
fullTitle:
FluxOps: Self-Healing Kubernetes Infrastructure Management
            Agent
description:
An LLM-powered infrastructure agent that monitors
            Kubernetes clusters in real-time, diagnoses performance degradation and outages using root cause analysis,
            and autonomously executes remediation actions — replacing L1/L2 on-call SRE workflows with zero human
            escalation for 80% of common incident types.

detailedDescription.problem:
Cloud infrastructure complexity has outpaced human operational capacity. The average enterprise runs 50–200 Kubernetes deployments across multiple cloud providers, with thousands of microservices generating millions of metrics and logs per second. When a service degrades, an on-call engineer must:

1. Receive an alert at 3 AM

2. Correlate metrics across Prometheus, Grafana, and CloudWatch

3. Triage the root cause from hundreds of potential failure points

4. Execute remediation steps from a runbook

5. Validate recovery

6. Write a post-mortem

This cycle takes 30–90 minutes per incident, burns out engineers (the average on-call rotation causes 4–8 hours of sleep disruption per week), and costs companies $5,600–$9,000 per minute of downtime for revenue-generating applications. The SRE talent market is critically constrained: average SRE compensation is $180K–$280K, with 6-week hiring cycles.

FluxOps directly addresses this by making the first response fully autonomous: it observes, diagnoses, and remediates 80% of common Kubernetes incident types without human involvement.
detailedDescription.solution: FluxOps is a Kubernetes-native agent that continuously ingests cluster telemetry (metrics, logs, traces, events) into a real-time anomaly detection system. When anomalies are detected, a Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Agent builds a causal graph by correlating multi-signal data. A Remediation Planner generates an ordered list of safe remediation actions based on historical incident data and runbook knowledge. A Remediation Executor Agent applies actions step-by-step, validates each step's outcome, and rolls back if validation fails. The entire process is logged in a structured incident report with root cause, actions taken, and future prevention recommendations.
detailedDescription.architecture:
System Architecture:

Observation Layer:

- Prometheus Operator integration for metrics ingestion (CPU, memory, network, custom app metrics)

- Elasticsearch integration for log aggregation and real-time anomaly scoring

- OpenTelemetry traces for distributed request tracing

- Kubernetes Event Stream monitoring via kube-apiserver watch API

- Custom eBPF probes for kernel-level network and I/O monitoring

Anomaly Detection Engine:

An ensemble of three anomaly detectors runs continuously:

1. Statistical detector: ARIMA models per time-series metric detecting deviation from predicted behavior

2. Isolation Forest for multivariate anomaly detection across correlated metrics

3. LLM-based log anomaly detector: streaming log lines through an embedding model to detect semantic deviation from normal log patterns

When any detector triggers, all three signals are combined into a composite anomaly score. Scores above threshold trigger the RCA Agent.

Root Cause Analysis Agent:

Builds a dynamic dependency graph of all services in the cluster using kube-apiserver metadata. Applies a probabilistic causal inference model (based on Pearl's do-calculus) to identify the most likely root cause service/component. Cross-references against a vector database of 10,000+ past incident root causes. Classifies the incident into one of 85 predefined incident categories (OOMKilled pod, network partition, database connection exhaustion, config map drift, certificate expiry, etc.).

Remediation Planning Agent:

For each incident category, maintains a decision tree of validated remediation actions with confidence scores. Actions are ranked by: safety (can the action cause further degradation?), effectiveness (historical success rate), and reversibility (can it be undone?). Plans are validated against a "blast radius" checker that ensures proposed actions won't cascade to other services.

Remediation Executor:

Executes Kubernetes API actions (kubectl equivalents via Python kubernetes-client library):

- Pod restarts with progressive rollout verification

- Horizontal Pod Autoscaler adjustments

- ConfigMap and Secret updates

- Node cordon/drain operations

- Helm chart rollbacks

- Service mesh traffic weight adjustments (Istio/Linkerd)

Each action is wrapped in a pre/post validation step: metrics must improve within a configured time window, or the action is rolled back.

Human Escalation Gateway:

If confidence in the diagnosis is below threshold, or if a proposed action exceeds configured risk levels, the system generates a full incident dossier (root cause hypothesis, evidence, proposed actions) and notifies the on-call engineer via PagerDuty with all context needed for immediate decision — reducing time to resolution from 48 minutes to <5 minutes even for escalated incidents.

Post-Incident Intelligence:

After every resolved incident, generates a structured blameless post-mortem document, updates the incident vector database for future RCA improvements, and generates prevention recommendations (alert threshold tuning, scaling policy updates, capacity planning).

title: LexOS
fullTitle:
LexOS: Enterprise Legal Document Intelligence and Contract Risk
            Platform
description:
A legal-domain RAG platform that ingests an
            enterprise's entire contract library, clause repositories, and regulatory documents, then enables lawyers to
            query, compare, extract, and risk-score contracts while automatically flagging non-standard clauses, missing
            protections, and regulatory compliance gaps — replacing months of manual contract review with seconds.

detailedDescription.problem:
The enterprise legal function is overwhelmed. A typical in-house legal team at a 500-person company manages 5,000–15,000 active contracts covering everything from enterprise software licenses to real estate leases to vendor service agreements. When a new contract arrives for review, an attorney must read 20–60 pages, compare it to the company's standard playbook, identify deviations, research relevant precedent, flag risk clauses, and draft redlines — a process taking 2–8 hours per contract at $200–$400/hour in-house or $400–$800/hour at external law firms.

For high-volume contract scenarios (M&A due diligence, annual vendor renewal season, real estate portfolio review), the economics become catastrophic: reviewing 200 contracts for an acquisition target can cost $400K–$1.6M in attorney fees and 4–6 weeks of elapsed time — often the critical path for closing a deal.

The alternative — rushing reviews or skipping them — leads to $2.3M average dispute costs and regulatory fines that dwarf the review cost. The legal technology market is $27.6B and growing at 9% CAGR, yet most tools only provide search, not intelligence.
detailedDescription.solution: LexOS is a legal-domain RAG platform purpose-built for contract intelligence. It ingests the enterprise's entire contract library (PDF, DOCX, scanned images via OCR), structures contracts into a hierarchical clause database, builds a semantic search index over all clause language, and enables attorneys to conduct sophisticated queries like "Show me all contracts with automatic renewal clauses where the notice period is less than 60 days" or "Compare this new vendor agreement's indemnification language to our standard playbook and highlight all deviations." The platform includes a Contract Risk Scorer that uses a trained classification model to rate risk across 15 dimensions (indemnification scope, limitation of liability caps, IP ownership, termination rights, governing law, etc.) and flags non-standard clauses with red/yellow/green indicators. It also includes an AI Contract Drafting Assistant that generates first-draft redlines in the attorney's own writing style.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Core Technical Architecture:

Document Ingestion Pipeline:

Accepts PDF, DOCX, scanned images (via Tesseract OCR + LayoutLM for structure recovery), and email attachments. Uses a multi-stage processing pipeline:

1. Document classification (contract type: MSA, NDA, employment, lease, SaaS, etc.)

2. Party and date extraction using NER (Named Entity Recognition with SpaCy + custom legal model)

3. Section segmentation: divides contracts into logical sections (recitals, definitions, obligations, warranties, indemnification, etc.) using a fine-tuned section classifier

4. Clause extraction: each section is further divided into individual clauses and stored as atomic units with metadata (section, clause type, party obligations, defined terms referenced)

5. Embedding generation: each clause is embedded using a legal-domain embedding model (fine-tuned on ContractNLI dataset) and stored in Qdrant

Clause Database Schema (PostgreSQL):

- contracts table: contract_id, type, parties, effective_date, expiry_date, governing_law, document_path

- clauses table: clause_id, contract_id, section, clause_type, text, risk_score, is_standard, deviation_from_playbook

- obligations table: obligation_id, clause_id, obligor_party, action_required, deadline, condition

Contract Risk Scoring Engine:

A multi-label classifier trained on 50,000+ annotated contract clauses from public EDGAR filings and legal datasets scores each clause across 15 risk dimensions. Risk scores are aggregated into a contract-level risk heat map. Non-standard clauses (those deviating >2 standard deviations from the company's historical playbook baseline) are automatically flagged.

Query and Intelligence Layer:

FastAPI backend exposing:

- Natural language contract query: "Find all contracts expiring in the next 90 days with auto-renewal provisions"

- Clause comparison: semantic diff between a new contract's clause and the standard playbook equivalent

- Obligation extraction: "List all payment obligations due in Q4 from all active vendor contracts"

- Risk aggregation: portfolio-level risk dashboard across all active contracts

RAG Question Answering:

For complex legal questions, a RAG pipeline retrieves the 10 most relevant clauses (semantic similarity + keyword filters), passes them to GPT-4o with a legal QA system prompt, and returns a structured answer with clause citations, confidence score, and attorney verification flag.

Contract Drafting Assistant:

Fine-tuned on the company's approved contract playbook, past redlines, and negotiation outcomes. When a new contract is uploaded, generates suggested redlines as tracked changes in DOCX format, with a brief explanation of each change rationale. Attorneys can accept/reject suggestions with one click.

title: MedRAG
fullTitle: MedRAG: Clinical Evidence Synthesis Engine for Physicians
description:
A medical-domain RAG system that ingests 35+
            million PubMed abstracts, clinical guidelines from 200+ medical societies, drug interaction databases, and
            the physician's own patient records, then answers complex clinical questions with evidence-graded responses
            citing specific studies, guidelines, and contraindications — giving every physician the equivalent of a
            specialist consultant at their side.

detailedDescription.problem:
Clinical medicine operates at the intersection of overwhelming evidence volume and extreme time pressure. PubMed adds 4,000+ new research articles every day; there are over 35 million indexed abstracts. Clinical guidelines from 200+ specialty societies are updated continuously. Simultaneously, a primary care physician sees 20–25 patients in a 9-hour day, leaving an average of 13 minutes per patient encounter — insufficient time to consult the literature for complex cases.

The result: guideline adherence in clinical practice is estimated at only 55–60%. Drug-drug interaction errors cause 125,000+ deaths and 1M+ hospitalizations annually in the US. Antibiotic resistance is accelerated by 30% overprescription driven by physician uncertainty. Each preventable adverse event costs the healthcare system $2,000–$50,000 in additional treatment costs.

MedRAG directly addresses this by compressing the evidence review process from 2–4 hours to under 10 seconds, with source-cited, evidence-graded answers that integrate seamlessly into the physician's workflow.
detailedDescription.solution: MedRAG maintains a continuously updated knowledge base comprising: PubMed abstracts (35M+, updated daily), clinical guidelines from 200+ societies (AHA, ACC, IDSA, etc.), FDA drug labels and interaction databases, ICD-10/SNOMED-CT/LOINC medical ontologies, and institution-specific clinical protocols. When a physician poses a clinical question ("What is the current evidence on empagliflozin in patients with CKD stage 3b and HFrEF who are already on spironolactone?"), MedRAG retrieves semantically relevant evidence chunks, synthesizes a response using a medically fine-tuned LLM, grades each recommendation by evidence level (A/B/C/D), and cites every specific guideline or study referenced.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Knowledge Base Construction:

PubMed Ingestion:

- Daily batch download of new PubMed abstracts via E-utilities API

- Full-text retrieval for open-access articles via PubMed Central OAI-PMH

- Structured extraction: PICO framework (Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome) extraction using BioBERT-based NLP pipeline

- Evidence grading: study type classification (RCT > meta-analysis > cohort > case series) for automatic evidence level assignment

Clinical Guidelines Processing:

- Web scraping + PDF extraction from 200+ medical society websites

- Structured guideline parsing: recommendation extraction with class of recommendation (I/IIa/IIb/III) and level of evidence (A/B/C)

- Version tracking: when guidelines are updated, old versions are archived and new versions take precedence with a change summary

Drug Database Integration:

- FDA DrugBank API integration for drug interactions, contraindications, dosing adjustments

- Epocrates-style drug interaction matrix updated weekly

- Renally/hepatically adjusted dosing tables for 2,000+ medications

Medical Knowledge Graph:

A Neo4j graph database modeling relationships between: diseases, drugs, procedures, biomarkers, patient populations, outcomes, and supporting evidence. Enables multi-hop reasoning queries like "What drugs approved for X condition have evidence for Y outcome in Z population?"

RAG Retrieval Pipeline:

- Hybrid retrieval: dense retrieval (MedCPT embeddings, purpose-built for medical text) + sparse BM25 with medical terminology expansion

- Query expansion: medical synonyms and related terms added using UMLS MetaMap

- Reranking: cross-encoder reranker fine-tuned on medical Q&A pairs for final passage selection

- Context assembly: retrieved passages organized by evidence hierarchy

Answer Generation:

GPT-4o with MedRAG system prompt generates structured responses following SBAR format (Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation) with mandatory citation format. Each recommendation tagged with evidence grade. Hallucination mitigation: all specific claims must have a retrieved supporting passage with semantic similarity > 0.75.

EHR Integration:

HL7 FHIR API integration allows MedRAG to automatically ingest patient demographics, diagnoses, medications, and lab values, enabling context-aware clinical queries without manual data entry.

title: FinRAG
fullTitle:
FinRAG: Real-Time Financial Regulatory Compliance Intelligence
            Platform
description:
A financial regulatory RAG platform that ingests
            the complete corpus of SEC rules, FINRA guidance, Fed regulations, CFTC rules, Basel III/IV frameworks, and
            50-state money transmission laws, then provides compliance officers with instant, source-cited answers to
            regulatory questions and auto-generates compliant policies, disclosures, and filings.

detailedDescription.problem:
The financial regulatory environment is the most complex text corpus on Earth for practical decision-making. US financial regulation alone spans: 10,000+ pages of Dodd-Frank Act rules, 40,000+ FINRA rules and guidance notices, 5,000+ pages of Federal Reserve Regulation, 2,000+ CFTC rules, SEC's 17 CFR Parts 200–399 (600+ sections), the complete Basel III/IV framework from BIS, 50 state-specific money transmission laws, and annual exam manuals from OCC, FDIC, and CFPB.

New regulations, no-action letters, guidance updates, and enforcement actions are published daily. A compliance officer at a mid-size broker-dealer receives 50–100 regulatory communications per month requiring review and assessment of impact on business operations.

The cost of getting it wrong: regulatory fines in financial services reached $10.4B globally in 2024. JP Morgan paid $290M for regulatory violations in 2024 alone. A 20-person compliance team at a boutique investment bank costs $3M–$5M annually — primarily for work that sophisticated AI can perform at a fraction of the cost.
detailedDescription.solution: FinRAG is a continuously updated regulatory intelligence platform that maintains a structured knowledge base of all major US and EU financial regulations. Compliance officers query it in natural language: "Does our proposed crypto custody service require a BitLicense in New York, and what are the capital requirements?" The system retrieves exact regulatory text, cross-references with recent enforcement actions, and generates a structured compliance analysis with specific citations, risk assessment, and next-step recommendations. It also generates first drafts of compliance policies, disclosures, and regulatory filings.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Regulatory Knowledge Base Architecture:

Document Ingestion:

- Federal Register API: automated ingestion of all new rule proposals and final rules

- EDGAR full-text search: SEC enforcement actions, no-action letters, staff guidance

- FINRA Rulebook API: complete rule set with amendment history

- BIS website scraper: Basel framework documents and consultation papers

- State regulatory portals: 50-state money transmission license requirements (scraped and structured)

- Regulatory update monitoring: RSS feeds from 40+ regulatory agencies with LLM-based impact classification

Document Processing Pipeline:

1. Document type classification: rule, guidance, enforcement action, FAQ, exam manual

2. Regulatory hierarchy mapping: rules are linked to their enabling statute, implementing regulations, and agency interpretations

3. Effective date tracking: multi-version document management showing how rules evolved

4. Cross-reference resolution: when Rule X references Rule Y, the relationship is stored in the knowledge graph

5. Jurisdiction tagging: each rule tagged with applicable jurisdiction (federal, state, product type, entity type)

Compliance Query Engine:

- Hybrid retrieval: regulatory-tuned embeddings (fine-tuned on 50,000 regulatory Q&A pairs) + BM25 with regulatory vocabulary

- Multi-hop retrieval: when a regulation references another, both are retrieved and contextually assembled

- Jurisdiction filtering: automatically applies state, product type, and entity type filters based on the query context

- Temporal relevance: shows the current version of rules, with option to view historical versions

Answer Generation with Citation Precision:

Responses structured as formal legal analysis memoranda:

- Issue identification

- Applicable regulatory sections (with exact CFR citations)

- Analysis of applicability to the described fact pattern

- Required actions and timelines

- Risk level assessment (Low/Medium/High/Critical)

- Related enforcement actions as precedent

Policy and Document Drafting:

A fine-tuned drafting model generates:

- Compliance policies (AML, BSA, OFAC screening, data privacy)

- Customer disclosures (Reg B, Reg Z, TILA, RESPA)

- Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) narratives

- Regulatory examination response letters

All in the appropriate regulatory format with standard language.

title: SynthDataForge
fullTitle:
SynthDataForge: Enterprise Synthetic Training Data Generation
            Pipeline
description:
A generative AI pipeline that produces
            statistically verified, privacy-compliant synthetic datasets for AI training — including tabular data, text
            corpora, image collections, and time-series — that preserve the statistical properties and distribution of
            real data while eliminating PII exposure, enabling AI model training without regulatory risk.

detailedDescription.problem:
The global AI training data market is $3.1B and projected to reach $12.9B by 2030 — a clear signal of how desperate AI teams are for high-quality labeled data. The core problem manifests in three critical scenarios:

Healthcare AI: A hospital wants to train a sepsis prediction model on ICU patient data. The data exists in their EHR, but HIPAA compliance makes sharing with external ML teams impossible. They need 50,000 de-identified patient records with preserved clinical relationships — but simple anonymization destroys statistical validity.

Financial AI: A bank wants to train a fraud detection model on transaction data. The real fraud cases are 0.01% of transactions — far too few for robust training. They need 100,000 synthetic fraud cases that match the statistical patterns of real fraud without being traceable to specific customers.

Autonomous Vehicles: A self-driving car company needs 10M+ labeled images of pedestrians in unusual scenarios (children playing near roads, wheelchairs on highways) that rarely appear in real-world data collection at acceptable cost.

In all these cases, synthetic data — generated to match the statistical properties of real data without being real data — is the solution. SynthDataForge automates this generation at enterprise scale with formal privacy guarantees.
detailedDescription.solution: SynthDataForge is a multi-modal synthetic data generation platform. For tabular data, it uses CTGAN (Conditional Tabular GAN) and TVAE models fine-tuned on the user's real data sample to generate statistically equivalent synthetic datasets with formal differential privacy guarantees (epsilon < 1). For text data, it uses domain-adapted LLMs to generate synthetic documents that match real document distributions. For images, it uses Stable Diffusion with ControlNet to generate photorealistic synthetic training images from text descriptions and reference samples. For time-series, it uses TimeGAN and transformer-based models. All outputs are validated with a Statistical Fidelity Report that measures how closely the synthetic data matches the real data on 20+ statistical dimensions.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Multi-Modal Generation Architecture:

Module 1 — Tabular Data Generation (CTGAN + DP-GAN):

Input: Sample of real tabular data (minimum 1,000 rows required)

Process:

1. Automatic data profiling: column types, distributions, correlations, missing value patterns

2. Privacy risk assessment: identifies quasi-identifiers and re-identification risk

3. Model selection: CTGAN for complex multi-modal distributions, TVAE for simpler datasets, DP-GAN for differential privacy requirement

4. Training with configurable DP epsilon (privacy budget): epsilon=1 provides strong privacy while preserving 85%+ statistical fidelity

5. Post-processing: enforce business rules and constraints (e.g., birth_date < admission_date)

6. Statistical fidelity validation using Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests, Wasserstein distance, and downstream ML performance comparison

Output: Synthetic tabular dataset + Statistical Fidelity Report (20 metrics)

Module 2 — Text Corpus Generation:

Input: Sample documents (minimum 500), topic taxonomy, output specifications

Process:

1. Document style analysis: sentence length distribution, vocabulary size, topic coverage, writing style embedding

2. Domain-specific language model fine-tuning using QLoRA on the sample corpus

3. Controlled generation with configurable topic, length, and style parameters

4. Coherence validation: generated documents scored on coherence, relevance, and style match

5. Diversity maximization: de-duplication using MinHash LSH to ensure sample diversity

Output: Synthetic text corpus with configurable size (1K–10M documents)

Module 3 — Synthetic Image Generation:

Input: Image category descriptions, reference samples, label schema

Process:

1. Stable Diffusion XL base model with domain fine-tuning using DreamBooth/LoRA

2. ControlNet integration for structural consistency (pose, edge, depth guidance)

3. Automated bounding box and segmentation label generation using SAM (Segment Anything Model)

4. Quality filtering using CLIP aesthetic score and FID (Fréchet Inception Distance) measurement

5. Dataset balancing: automatic generation of additional examples for underrepresented classes

Output: Annotated synthetic image dataset in COCO/YOLO format

Module 4 — Time-Series Generation:

Input: Historical time-series data, anomaly labels, generation parameters

Process:

1. Time-series decomposition: trend, seasonality, residual decomposition

2. TimeGAN training on the decomposed signal

3. Anomaly injection with configurable frequency and severity distributions

4. Statistical validation: autocorrelation preservation, spectral density comparison

Module 5 — Privacy Compliance Certification:

For all generated data types, runs a formal re-identification risk assessment:

- Membership inference attack simulation: attempts to determine if any synthetic record corresponds to a real individual

- Singling-out risk: identifies records that could uniquely identify an individual

- Generates a Privacy Compliance Certificate suitable for HIPAA Safe Harbor or GDPR Article 89 compliance documentation

Web Platform:

React-based workspace where users configure generation jobs, monitor progress, download outputs, and receive statistical validation reports. REST API for programmatic integration into ML pipelines.

title: DocuBot
fullTitle:
DocuBot: AI-Powered Technical Documentation Generator and
            Maintainer
description:
A codebase-aware AI system that reads an entire
            software repository, automatically generates comprehensive technical documentation (API references,
            architecture guides, onboarding tutorials, changelogs, and user manuals), keeps documentation in sync with
            code changes via CI/CD integration, and detects when code changes make existing documentation outdated.

detailedDescription.problem:
Software documentation exists on a spectrum from non-existent to catastrophically outdated. For every developer who ever copy-pasted code from Stack Overflow to understand an undocumented function, or spent an afternoon deciphering a colleague's "self-documenting code" — this problem is personal and universal.

At enterprise scale, the economics are staggering: a 50-engineer organization where each engineer spends 5 hours/week searching for undocumented code understanding costs 250 engineer-hours/week — approximately $500K/year at $200/hour. For new engineer onboarding, undocumented codebases add 3–6 weeks to the ramp-up period, costing $15K–$30K per new hire in lost productivity.

For open-source projects and commercial APIs, documentation quality directly correlates with adoption rates. The Stripe API achieved market dominance partially through its documentation quality. For every SaaS company with an API product, poor documentation is a direct revenue constraint.

DocuBot automates the documentation lifecycle end-to-end: generation, maintenance, and staleness detection.
detailedDescription.solution: DocuBot integrates into a software repository as a GitHub App or GitLab integration. On first installation, it reads the entire codebase using a repository-level understanding pipeline, generates comprehensive documentation across 8 categories (API reference, architecture guide, data model reference, deployment guide, onboarding tutorial, FAQ, changelog, and user manual), and publishes it to a configurable documentation site. When developers commit code changes, DocuBot automatically identifies which documentation sections are affected, regenerates them with the updated logic, and opens a pull request with documentation updates — keeping docs perpetually synchronized with code.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Repository Understanding Pipeline:

Phase 1 — Code Parsing and AST Analysis:

Supports 15 programming languages (Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Go, Rust, C++, Ruby, PHP, C#, Kotlin, Swift, Scala, R, SQL). For each language, uses the appropriate AST parser to extract:

- All public functions/methods with signatures, parameters, and return types

- All classes with inheritance hierarchies and public interfaces

- Configuration files and environment variables

- Database schema from ORM models (SQLAlchemy, Hibernate, Prisma, etc.)

- API route definitions from web frameworks (FastAPI, Express, Django, Rails, etc.)

Phase 2 — Semantic Code Understanding:

A code-understanding LLM (GPT-4o with code-optimized context window) processes each extracted code element with its surrounding context to generate:

- Plain-English description of what the function/class does

- Input/output contracts and data types

- Side effects, error conditions, and edge cases

- Code examples showing typical usage

Phase 3 — Documentation Generation (8 Categories):

1. API Reference: Complete function/class/endpoint documentation in ReadTheDocs/OpenAPI format

2. Architecture Guide: System component diagram description, data flow explanation, technology stack justification

3. Data Model Reference: Entity descriptions, relationships, constraints — generated from ORM schema analysis

4. Deployment Guide: Environment requirements, configuration variables, startup procedure — extracted from Dockerfile, docker-compose, CI config

5. Onboarding Tutorial: Step-by-step "Getting Started" guide for new developers, covering setup to first feature

6. FAQ: Generated from issue tracker analysis (GitHub Issues, Jira) — common problems and their solutions

7. Changelog: Auto-generated from git commit history, PR titles, and code diff analysis

8. User Manual: Feature descriptions in non-technical language for end users, generated from UI code analysis

Phase 4 — Documentation Site Generation:

Outputs to multiple formats: Markdown files (for Docusaurus/MkDocs), HTML static site, and interactive API playground (based on OpenAPI spec). Hosted on configurable platforms (GitHub Pages, Netlify, Vercel).

Phase 5 — Continuous Synchronization (The Core Innovation):

On every commit, a diff analysis identifies changed functions/classes. For each changed element, the previous documentation is retrieved, the new code is analyzed, and a documentation diff is generated. If the change significantly alters behavior, DocuBot opens a PR with updated documentation. Staleness Detection: uses semantic similarity between code and documentation to flag documentation that no longer accurately describes the code, even without explicit changes.

title: QualityEye
fullTitle:
QualityEye: Real-Time Manufacturing Defect Detection and Root
            Cause System
description:
A computer vision system deployed on manufacturing
            production lines that detects micro-defects in real-time at industrial speeds (200+ FPS), classifies defect
            types, traces each defect back to its machine, shift, material batch, and process parameter through causal
            inference, and provides adaptive quality control recommendations that reduce defect rates by 70–90%.

detailedDescription.problem:
Manufacturing quality control is simultaneously one of the most critical and most underdeveloped applications of AI. The problem is well-defined: detect defects in physical products before they leave the production line, at production speed, with near-zero false negatives (missed defects) and acceptable false positives (unnecessary rejections).

In a typical electronics PCB manufacturing line, production runs at 2,000–5,000 boards per hour. Each board has 200–5,000 solder joints, any one of which could exhibit voids, bridges, cold joints, or missing components. A human inspector viewing at 1 second/board can examine roughly 5–10 joints per board — missing 99%+ of potential defects. Automated Optical Inspection (AOI) systems from traditional vendors (Omron, Cognex, Keyence) cost $150,000–$500,000 per station, require 200+ hours of expert programming for each new product, and still generate 15–30% false positive rates that require human verification.

QualityEye solves all three problems: it runs on commodity industrial cameras ($5,000–$15,000), requires minimal configuration (learns from examples rather than programmed rules), achieves sub-0.1% false negative rate and <2% false positive rate, and adds a unique capability existing systems lack entirely: root cause analysis that traces defects back to their origin in the manufacturing process.
detailedDescription.solution: QualityEye deploys as an edge-computing system at each inspection station. A high-speed industrial camera captures images at production speed. An optimized YOLO-v10 model (quantized to INT8 for edge inference) detects and classifies defects in real-time with sub-5ms latency per frame. Each detected defect is logged with a full metadata payload: timestamp, production line ID, machine ID, operator shift, raw material batch number, and process parameters active at the time. A causal inference engine analyzes this multi-dimensional defect log to identify which process parameters (temperature, speed, pressure, material batch) are predictive of specific defect types. The system automatically generates process adjustment recommendations to reduce defect rates.
detailedDescription.architecture:
System Architecture:

Edge Hardware Setup:

- Industrial camera: 12MP resolution at 200 FPS (e.g., Basler acA4600-10gc) with telecentric lens for geometric distortion-free imaging

- Edge computing unit: NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier for INT8 model inference

- Illumination: configurable multi-angle LED arrays for optimal defect visibility (grazing, coaxial, dark-field modes)

- Trigger system: encoder-synchronized image capture with conveyor belt position tracking

Computer Vision Pipeline:

1. Image preprocessing: automatic white balance correction, lens distortion correction, perspective normalization

2. Region-of-Interest (ROI) detection: a lightweight detector identifies the product and warps it to a canonical reference frame using homographic transformation

3. Defect detection: YOLOv10-based detector with custom head for 50 defect class taxonomy

4. Anomaly detection: a parallel autoencoder-based anomaly scorer runs for novel defect types not in the training taxonomy — flags unusual patterns even when not classified

5. Defect severity scoring: localization + severity model assigns a criticality score (1–10) to each defect, enabling configurable rejection thresholds

Model Training and Adaptation:

- Base model pre-trained on industry defect datasets (DAGM, MVTec AD, custom industrial datasets)

- Few-shot adaptation: new product lines learned from 50–200 labeled examples using prototype network approach

- Continuous learning: production feedback (false positives confirmed by operators) is automatically incorporated in weekly model updates

- Synthetic data augmentation: GAN-based defect synthesis generates additional training examples for rare defect types

Process Data Integration (The Unique Differentiator):

QualityEye integrates with the factory's MES (Manufacturing Execution System), SCADA system, and IoT sensors to capture process parameters at the exact moment each defective unit was produced:

- Machine parameters: temperature, pressure, speed, vibration, power draw

- Material data: raw material batch ID, supplier, material properties from quality certificates

- Environmental: ambient temperature, humidity, shift, operator ID

This creates a time-aligned dataset of defect events and process parameters.

Root Cause Analysis Engine:

Applies causal inference algorithms (PC algorithm for Bayesian network learning, DoWhy for causal effect estimation) to identify which process parameters causally influence specific defect types. For example: "Defect type 'solder void' increases by 340% when solder paste temperature exceeds 27°C during application, independent of other variables."

Adaptive Control Recommendations:

The causal model generates specific, actionable recommendations with expected defect reduction:

- "Reduce paste application speed from 120mm/s to 90mm/s on Line 3 to reduce bridging defects by estimated 65%"

- "Replace material batch MBX-2241 — associated with 8x elevated void defect rate"

Dashboard and Alert System:

Real-time Grafana dashboard showing: current defect rate, defect distribution by type, process parameter correlation heat maps, and shift-by-shift performance comparison. Automatic OPC-UA signals to conveyor control for automatic rejection of detected defective units.

title: SkyAnalyst
fullTitle:
SkyAnalyst: Satellite Imagery Intelligence Platform for Asset
            Monitoring
description:
A satellite imagery analysis platform that uses
            multi-spectral deep learning models to monitor, count, classify, and track physical assets (vehicles, ships,
            construction equipment, oil tanks, agricultural machinery) from commercially available satellite imagery at
            global scale, providing actionable intelligence to commodities traders, insurance companies, and supply
            chain analysts.

detailedDescription.problem:
Information asymmetry is the foundation of profitable trading and supply chain decision-making. Physical world data — the actual quantity of goods produced, stored, and transported — is the most valuable and least accessible form of market intelligence. Traditional sources (company reports, government statistics) are delayed by 30–90 days and subject to manipulation. Real-time physical intelligence derived from satellite imagery provides ground truth about economic activity before any official report.

The addressable use cases are enormous:

- Commodities trading: oil tank volume estimation from shadow analysis tells you actual crude oil inventory before EIA reports (worth millions in trading positions)

- Agricultural intelligence: crop yield estimation from vegetation indices tells insurance companies and food traders harvest estimates 3 months before harvest

- Maritime intelligence: ship traffic monitoring in Strait of Malacca or Suez Canal provides real-time global trade volume data

- Insurance assessment: post-disaster damage assessment from satellite imagery enables rapid claims processing and fraud detection

- Competitive intelligence: monitoring competitor factory activity from vehicle counts and parking lot density

Existing solutions (Planet Labs, Maxar, Orbital Insight) charge $50K–$500K/year and require specialized geospatial analysts. SkyAnalyst makes this intelligence accessible to mid-market financial and supply chain teams at 10–20% of the cost.
detailedDescription.solution: SkyAnalyst is an automated satellite imagery analysis platform. Users define Areas of Interest (AOIs) and monitoring tasks (count vehicles in parking lot, estimate oil tank fill level, measure crop vegetation index). The platform automatically schedules satellite imagery acquisition from commercial providers (Planet Labs, Sentinel-2, SPOT), runs domain-specific deep learning models on new imagery, and delivers structured intelligence reports with time-series charts showing how the monitored assets change over time.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Technical Architecture:

Imagery Acquisition Layer:

- Planet Labs API: daily 3m-resolution imagery, worldwide coverage

- Sentinel-2 (ESA): free 10m-resolution multi-spectral imagery, 5-day revisit

- SPOT (Airbus): 1.5m-resolution commercial imagery for high-precision tasks

- Custom acquisition scheduler: automatically orders imagery for active AOIs on configurable schedules

Image Processing Pipeline:

1. Radiometric calibration: converts raw digital numbers to surface reflectance using atmospheric correction (py6S library for Sentinel-2, metadata-based for Planet)

2. Orthorectification: removes terrain-induced distortions using DEM (Digital Elevation Model) data

3. Cloud masking: ML-based cloud and shadow detection (s2cloudless library + custom CNN)

4. Multi-temporal stacking: aligns imagery from different dates for change detection analysis

5. Tiling: divides large AOIs into 512x512 pixel tiles for model inference

Domain-Specific Intelligence Models:

Each intelligence use case has a dedicated model:

1. Vehicle Detection and Counting:

- RetinaNet-based object detector fine-tuned on DigitalGlobe's labeled vehicle dataset + augmented with synthetic vehicles

- Achieves F1 > 0.85 on medium-resolution (3m) imagery

- Vehicle classification: car, truck, semi-trailer, construction equipment, aircraft

- Parking lot capacity estimation: compare detected vehicles to maximum lot capacity

2. Oil Tank Volume Estimation:

- Floating roof tank fill level estimation from shadow analysis (established geometry method)

- Fixed roof tank detection and capacity database built from known refinery locations

- Automated volume calculation: tank diameter + shadow length → fill percentage

3. Agricultural Crop Analysis:

- Vegetation index computation: NDVI, EVI, SAVI from multi-spectral bands

- Crop type classification using time-series signature (field-level CNN + LSTM for temporal pattern)

- Yield prediction: regression model mapping NDVI temporal profile to historical yield data

- Phenological stage detection: planting, vegetative, flowering, maturity, harvest

4. Ship Detection and Classification:

- SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) + optical fusion for all-weather ship detection

- Ship classification: tanker, container, bulk carrier, warship — using hull geometry features

- AIS correlation: match detected ships to AIS transponder data for identification

5. Construction Activity Monitoring:

- Change detection model comparing multi-date imagery

- Material pile volume estimation from shadow analysis

- Equipment presence detection (crane, excavator, concrete truck)

Intelligence Delivery Layer:

FastAPI backend serving structured JSON intelligence reports with time-series data. React dashboard with interactive maps (Mapbox GL), time-series charts (Recharts), and alert management. Automated daily email briefings with AI-narrated summaries of changes.

title: SentimentGrid
fullTitle:
SentimentGrid: Real-Time Multi-Source Financial Sentiment
            Intelligence Engine
description:
A real-time financial sentiment analysis platform
            that aggregates news, earnings call transcripts, social media, analyst reports, and regulatory filings
            across 10,000+ assets, applies domain-specific NLP models to extract directional sentiment signals, and
            delivers quantified, backtest-validated trading signals with 15-minute latency to systematic trading firms.

detailedDescription.problem:
Alternative data is one of the fastest-growing segments of the financial data industry, projected to reach $17.4B by 2027. Sentiment signals derived from text — a sub-category of alternative data — are among the most widely used by quantitative funds. Studies by Renault (2017), Chen et al. (2014), and multiple academic teams confirm that earnings call sentiment, news sentiment, and social media sentiment are predictive of short-term equity returns with statistical significance.

The problem: building a production sentiment pipeline is a major engineering undertaking. Data sources are heterogeneous (structured, unstructured, semi-structured), update at different frequencies (real-time, hourly, daily), require different parsing logic (PDF earnings transcripts vs. RSS news feeds vs. Twitter API vs. 8-K filings), and must be processed with financial-domain NLP models that outperform generic sentiment models by 15–30% on financial text.

The buy side needs this as a service: a clean, latency-optimized, backtest-validated API that delivers normalized sentiment scores they can plug directly into their signal research frameworks.
detailedDescription.solution: SentimentGrid is a data platform that maintains 50+ financial text sources, processes them through a financial-domain NLP pipeline (FinBERT + custom trained models), and delivers normalized sentiment scores per ticker/asset with 15-minute maximum latency. Every signal includes: raw score, normalized score vs. 90-day baseline, confidence level, source attribution, and a historical backtest showing the signal's relationship to subsequent price moves over multiple time horizons.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Data Collection Infrastructure:

Source Categories and Ingestion Methods:

1. Financial News: RSS feeds from Reuters, Bloomberg (free tier), MarketWatch, Seeking Alpha, Business Wire, PR Newswire — 12,000+ articles/day parsed via Feedparser + LLM headline cleaning

2. SEC Filings: EDGAR full-text API — 8-K, 10-Q, 10-K, DEF 14A processed within 5 minutes of filing

3. Earnings Call Transcripts: Automated download from Seeking Alpha, Motley Fool — parsed from HTML with speaker attribution

4. Analyst Reports: Research report aggregators via API (requires licensing), PDF extraction for content

5. Social Media: Reddit (r/wallstreetbets, r/investing) via Pushshift API, Twitter/X Firehose API for filtered financial mentions

6. Corporate Blogs and IR Pages: Monitored via change detection with daily scraping

NLP Processing Pipeline:

Each text document passes through a multi-stage NLP pipeline:

Stage 1 — Asset Attribution:

Custom NER model identifies ticker symbols, company names, product names, and executive names mentioned in each document. Disambiguation resolves "Apple" (AAPL) vs. generic "apple." Multi-ticker documents generate separate sentiment scores per mentioned entity.

Stage 2 — Relevance Filtering:

A binary classifier determines whether a document has investment-relevant content for each mentioned ticker. Filters out tangential mentions ("Apple donated to the Red Cross" is not investment-relevant for AAPL).

Stage 3 — Sentiment Classification:

FinBERT (BERT fine-tuned on 10,000 financial sentences by Prosus NLP) classifies each relevant passage as Positive/Negative/Neutral with confidence scores. For earnings calls, separate models target: forward guidance sentiment, management tone (evasiveness/confidence), analyst Q&A tension score.

Stage 4 — Aspect-Based Sentiment (ABS):

For longer documents (10-K, earnings transcripts), aspect-based sentiment extraction identifies sentiment specifically about: revenue outlook, margin trajectory, competitive positioning, regulatory risk, management confidence, product pipeline. Each aspect generates a separate signal.

Stage 5 — Signal Normalization:

Raw sentiment scores are normalized against a rolling 90-day baseline for each ticker. This adjusts for systematic biases (news about certain sectors is chronically more negative) and produces a standardized Z-score that is comparable across assets and source types.

Backtesting Framework:

For each signal type, the platform maintains an expanding-window backtest showing the signal's information coefficient (IC) — correlation with next-day, next-week, and next-month returns — across different market regimes. Signal decay curves show how quickly each signal type fades.

API and Delivery:

REST API: query sentiment scores for any ticker at any historical time, with attribution breakdown by source type

WebSocket stream: real-time signal updates with sub-15-minute latency

Bulk delivery: daily CSV/parquet files for research workflows

Backtest integration: SDK for common research environments (Zipline, QuantConnect, Alphalens)

title: MeetingMind
fullTitle:
MeetingMind: Autonomous Meeting Intelligence and Action
            Execution Platform
description:
A real-time meeting analysis platform that
            transcribes, understands, and acts on meeting content — extracting action items, decisions, open questions,
            and commitments, assigning them to calendar events and project management tasks, drafting follow-up emails,
            updating CRM records, and generating structured meeting summaries — reducing post-meeting administrative
            work by 90%.

detailedDescription.problem:
Meetings are simultaneously the most expensive and least productive use of knowledge worker time. The average meeting produces:

- 5–12 action items, 60–80% of which are never completed due to poor tracking

- 3–7 decisions, of which participants have conflicting recollections within 48 hours

- 2–4 open questions that require follow-up research, typically forgotten

- 1–3 verbal commitments ("I'll send you the report by Thursday") that have no tracking mechanism

The post-meeting administrative cascade — writing notes, creating Jira tickets, scheduling follow-up calls, updating Salesforce opportunities — consumes 1–3 hours for every hour of meeting time. For a sales organization where meeting notes in CRM are mandatory, this overhead directly constrains the number of deals a rep can manage.

MeetingMind automates the entire post-meeting workflow: not just transcription and summarization, but the downstream task creation, calendar scheduling, email drafting, and CRM updating that converts meeting insights into organizational action.
detailedDescription.solution: MeetingMind integrates into video conferencing platforms (Zoom, Teams, Google Meet) as a bot participant. It performs real-time transcription with speaker diarization, applies NLP models to extract structured intelligence (action items, decisions, questions, commitments, key topics), and automatically executes the downstream workflow: creating Jira/Asana tasks, updating Salesforce, scheduling follow-up meetings, drafting recap emails, and generating structured meeting records in the team's documentation system.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Real-Time Processing Pipeline:

Audio Ingestion:

- Zoom/Teams/Meet bot integration via official bot APIs

- Real-time audio streaming via WebSocket to transcription service

- Whisper Large v3 for high-accuracy transcription with sub-2-second latency

- Speaker diarization using pyannote.audio with multi-speaker models

- Custom vocabulary injection: company-specific terminology, product names, acronyms

Structured Information Extraction:

A multi-task NLP model processes the transcript in real-time, running 6 extraction tasks simultaneously:

1. Action Item Extraction:

Identifies sentences containing commitments to future action. Pattern: [Person] will/is going to/should [action] by [optional deadline].

Output: structured action item with assignee, task description, deadline, and context.

2. Decision Logging:

Identifies consensus statements and decision announcements.

Output: decision text, decision-makers present, related context.

3. Open Question Tracking:

Identifies questions posed that didn't receive definitive answers during the meeting.

Output: question text, asker, status (answered/open).

4. Key Topic Extraction:

LDA-based topic modeling + NER to identify the main discussion themes, mentioned entities (companies, products, people), and key metrics discussed.

5. Commitment Tracking:

Identifies informal commitments ("I'll get back to you by end of week") — distinct from formal action items in that they may not be explicitly tasked but represent accountability.

6. Meeting Quality Analysis:

Tracks: meeting duration, talk-time distribution by speaker, energy level changes (enthusiasm/concern detection), number of interruptions.

Post-Meeting Execution Engine:

After meeting ends (or configurable during meeting), an Execution Agent runs:

1. Task Creation:

For each action item, creates a task in the configured project management tool (Jira/Asana/Linear/ClickUp) with: title, description, assignee, due date, meeting link, and meeting context. Uses user's task template preferences.

2. Calendar Actions:

For follow-up meeting requests identified in meeting ("Let's sync on this next Tuesday"), sends calendar invites with the meeting summary as description.

3. Email Drafting:

Generates a structured meeting recap email using the company's email style guide (learned from past recap emails). Draft is presented for human review with one-click send.

4. CRM Update:

For sales meetings, extracts: next steps, deal stage changes, stakeholder sentiment, competitive mentions, timeline updates. Auto-fills Salesforce opportunity record.

5. Documentation Update:

Appends structured meeting record to Confluence, Notion, or SharePoint with: attendees, decisions, action items, and full transcript searchable by keyword.

Meeting Analytics Dashboard:

Historical meeting analysis showing: average meeting ROI score, action item completion rate by person, topic distribution over time, talking-time equity metrics.

title: DarkShield
fullTitle:
DarkShield: Autonomous Dark Web Credential and Threat
            Intelligence Monitor
description:
A fully automated threat intelligence system that
            continuously monitors dark web forums, paste sites, breach databases, and criminal marketplaces for leaked
            corporate credentials, intellectual property, and attack planning activity, then immediately triggers
            automated remediation workflows — credential resets, access revocations, and security team alerts — before
            breaches become incidents.

detailedDescription.problem:
The dark web is a persistent, real-time feed of corporate attack planning. Criminal forums like XSS.is, Exploit.in, and RaidForums (and its successors) host active trading of corporate credentials, zero-day exploits, and targeted attack discussions. Paste sites (Pastebin, ghostbin, rentry) receive thousands of credential dumps daily. Underground marketplaces sell access to corporate networks for $500–$10,000 per compromised account.

Most companies have no visibility into this world. By the time a stolen credential appears in a publicly indexed breach database (like Have I Been Pwned), it's been in criminal hands for an average of 18 months. The 9-hour window between theft and use is the critical intervention opportunity — and only automated monitoring can catch it.

Companies that do invest in threat intelligence face prohibitive costs: Recorded Future costs $250K–$500K/year; Intel 471 starts at $200K. Professional SOC (Security Operations Center) teams to act on the intelligence cost another $500K–$2M annually. DarkShield brings this capability to mid-market companies at $5,000–$25,000/month.
detailedDescription.solution: DarkShield is a continuously running automated threat intelligence and response system. It maintains a TOR-based crawler network that monitors 500+ dark web forums, paste sites, and marketplace listings, applies NLP and pattern matching to identify mentions of the client company's domains, email addresses, IP ranges, and key personnel. When threats are detected, an automated response pipeline triggers: credential invalidation via Active Directory/Okta API, security team alerts with context, and executive briefings. All activity is synthesized into a daily threat intelligence brief.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Architecture (Security-First Design):

Crawler Infrastructure:

Runs entirely through TOR network with rotating exit nodes to maintain operational security. Multiple independent crawler instances for redundancy and scale. Uses Playwright + stealth plugins for JavaScript-rendered dark web content. Implements randomized crawl delays and human-like browsing patterns to avoid detection.

Monitored Sources:

- Dark web forums: 200+ indexed forums (criminal discussion, malware trading, data brokering)

- Paste sites: Pastebin, 0bin, ghostbin, hastebin — real-time monitoring via API + scraping

- Breach databases: HaveIBeenPwned API, DeHashed, Snusbase for indexed historical breaches

- Underground marketplaces: automated monitoring of 30+ known criminal markets

- Telegram channels: 500+ cybercriminal Telegram channels via MTProto API

- GitHub/GitLab: monitoring for accidentally committed secrets (tokens, passwords) matching client domains

Threat Detection Engine:

Multi-stage detection pipeline:

1. Pattern Matching Layer:

Pre-compiled regex patterns for client-specific identifiers: email domains (@company.com), internal IP ranges, domain names, executive names, product names, API key formats.

2. Fuzzy Matching:

Handles typosquatted domains (company.corn, c0mpany.com) and deliberate obfuscation (c[dot]ompany[dot]com).

3. Contextual NLP Analysis:

For matching documents, an LLM analysis determines:

- Threat type classification: credential theft, targeted attack planning, IP leakage, executive targeting, infrastructure reconnaissance

- Threat actor profiling: is this from a known threat actor group?

- Urgency scoring: is this a historical exposure or active planning?

- Confidence score: how certain is the system this is a real threat?

4. Intelligence Enrichment:

For identified credentials: verify against internal identity provider whether credential is still active. For IP addresses: correlate against company's registered IP ranges. For attack plans: map to MITRE ATT&CK framework for response guidance.

Automated Response Engine:

Triggered by threats above configured confidence/severity thresholds:

1. Credential Response (< 15 minutes from detection):

- Query Okta/Azure AD to verify credential status

- If active: force password reset via identity provider API + trigger MFA enrollment

- Notify user with security advisory and instructions

- Log in SIEM (Splunk, QRadar) as security incident

2. Attack Planning Response:

- Notify CISO and SOC team with full context brief

- Generate incident response checklist based on threat type

- Automatically increase monitoring on mentioned assets

3. Data Leakage Response:

- Identify what data was leaked (credentials, PII, IP)

- Trigger GDPR/CCPA breach notification workflow if PII is involved

- Initiate legal hold and forensic evidence preservation

Threat Intelligence Dashboard:

React dashboard showing: active threats, threat trend history, exposure score, remediated vs. open threats, dark web mention timeline, threat actor profile library.

Daily Threat Brief:

Auto-generated executive summary in PDF/email format: "In the past 24 hours, we detected 3 new threats against your organization: [summary]. Actions taken: [summary]. Recommended actions: [summary]."

title: ProcessMiner
fullTitle:
ProcessMiner: Enterprise Business Process Discovery and RPA
            Code Generator
description:
An AI system that passively observes user desktop
            activity across an organization to automatically discover, map, and document all manual business processes,
            then generates production-ready RPA (Robotic Process Automation) code that automates the discovered
            processes — without requiring business analysts or RPA developers.

detailedDescription.problem:
The Robotic Process Automation market is $3.6B and growing at 23% CAGR, driven by enterprises desperately trying to automate repetitive manual tasks. Yet 30–50% of RPA projects fail, and average implementation costs of $150K–$500K put automation out of reach for most processes.

The core problem: process discovery. Before any automation can be written, someone must observe and document exactly how humans perform each process — every click, every copy-paste, every conditional decision. This "task mining" phase requires dedicated business analysts interviewing employees, shadowing sessions, and months of process mapping work. For a company with 200 manual processes, this is a 5-10 year project with a 50-person BA team.

The innovation gap: no existing tool can both discover processes from raw behavioral data AND generate production automation code automatically. RPA vendors (UiPath, Automation Anywhere) sell discovery tools and code generators as separate $200K+ products. ProcessMiner integrates both into a single automated pipeline.
detailedDescription.solution: ProcessMiner deploys a lightweight desktop agent on knowledge worker computers (with full consent and transparency). The agent captures screen state, keystrokes, application switching, and workflow decisions, but immediately processes this data locally and transmits only structured process events (not raw screenshots or keystrokes) to the central server. An AI process discovery engine automatically maps these event streams into Process Models (BPMN notation). A code generation engine then produces Python/UiPath automation scripts for each discovered process, complete with error handling and test cases.
detailedDescription.architecture:
Technical Architecture (Privacy-First Design):

Desktop Agent (Privacy-Preserving):
